column2: What is the name of the phenomenon that would make me choose A over B because I was influenced by a trend?  I've looked at these 1,2,3 and I don't think they answer my question fully.  Let's say it's election time and I'm on the fence about who to vote for. I see some survey on the news and seeing party A has more vote intentions, it tends to sway my decision towards party A.  Closer to home, I see a question or answer on SE and I may feel swayed to vote +1 or -1 if there are pre-existing votes that have been cast.  What would this phenomenon be called (if there is a name for it other than simple influence or conformity)? Also, can there be a bias for going against what the current trend is? What is the name of the phenomenon that would make me choose A over B because I was influenced by a trend?  I've looked at these 1,2,3 and I don't think they answer my question fully.  Let's say it's election time and I'm on the fence about who to vote for. I see some survey on the news and seeing party A has more vote intentions, it tends to sway my decision towards party A.  Closer to home, I see a question or answer on SE and I may feel swayed to vote +1 or -1 if there are pre-existing votes that have been cast.  What would this phenomenon be called (if there is a name for it other than simple influence or conformity)? Also, can there be a bias for going against what the current trend is? The phenomenon where individuals are swayed to choose A over B because others are choosing A is often called the 'bandwagon effect.' This bias causes people to align their beliefs or actions with those of the majority, either because they believe the majority is correct or want to fit in. In psychology, this relates to social conformity and herd behavior. There is also an opposite bias called the 'underdog effect' or 'reverse bandwagon effect,' where individuals are inclined to go against the prevailing trend.

That encompasses creating PowerPoint presentations, explaining the topic to someone or writing summaries. I think my active contribution is what matters here.  For a while, I used the term social memory but I've learned it refers rather to obervational learning. So is there a term for it? That encompasses creating PowerPoint presentations, explaining the topic to someone or writing summaries. I think my active contribution is what matters here.  For a while, I used the term social memory but I've learned it refers rather to obervational learning. So is there a term for it? The type of learning you are describing is known as 'active learning.' Active learning requires active engagement and contribution from the learner, such as organizing information into meaningful structures, creating presentations, explaining concepts to others, or writing summaries. These methods deepen understanding and improve memory retention by encouraging learners to process and manipulate information, rather than simply receiving it passively.

How does Kohlberg's moral reasoning work, can I measure it myself?  Is there any way I can work out what stage Kohlberg would claim I was operating at?  I do refer, when thinking about what is best, permissible, etc., to laws and other people's opinions, punishment etc., but not as conclusive judgments on what is right or wrong etc., only as one aspect of how to try to make that judgment myself. And while my actual judgments I would claim apply to anyone, not just myself, they are also provisional, and subject to revision, reappraisal.  With some exceptions:  My happiness is often more valuable than other's, maybe just because I'm especially well set to work on that. Likewise I wouldn't be obliged to martyr myself for any reason, e.g. to save a million lives, though I would say the same of anyone. And certain actions are always and unequivocally wrong, for anyone, they are that heinous.  I think enough and loosely enough about morality to say that I'm probably post conventional, and perhaps that's all that really does matter. But I wondered if I could learn more! Is there a formal name for the compulsive and recurrent urges to pick one's nose in order to remove perceived imperfections?  Under which formal category does the following condition fall?: the condition of experiencing frequent and compulsive urges to pick one's nose searching for any possible perceived imperfections (be they wet snot, dry snot and even scabs) in order to remove them or "smooth them out" by means of scratching, in a sort of endless pursuit for a sensation of perfect smoothness within one's nose (endless because the sensation of perfect smoothness is never quite achieved and usually the scratching itself leads to bleeding and scabs which paradoxically become the fuel for future nose picking sessions, in an endless cycle). References are very welcomed. Is there a formal name for the compulsive and recurrent urges to pick one's nose in order to remove perceived imperfections? References are very welcomed. The compulsive and recurrent urge to pick one's nose, especially in the way you've described (focusing on perceived imperfections and pursuing a sensation of smoothness), is called rhinotillexomania. This behavior is considered a subtype of body-focused repetitive behaviors (BFRBs), which are a group of obsessive-compulsive and related disorders. Other BFRBs include trichotillomania (hair-pulling) and dermatillomania (skin-picking). These conditions are categorized under the 'Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders' section in the DSM-5. My Mind Shows Me My Future [closed]  When I sleep, I can see some phenomenon as a dream. After some days or months or years that phenomenon happens in my life and forthwith my mind reminds me of that earlier dream that I saw. There are simple things such as for example getting down pen from my hand like wise.  My problem is how can this happen? I have heard this happens to others also. What kind of situation is this in the mind? Is it normal or abnormal? My Mind Shows Me My Future [closed]  When I sleep, I can see some phenomenon as a dream. After some days or months or years that phenomenon happens in my life and forthwith my mind reminds me of that earlier dream that I saw. There are simple things such as for example getting down pen from my hand like wise.  My problem is how can this happen? I have heard this happens to others also. What kind of situation is this in the mind? Is it normal or abnormal? The scientific explanation for experiencing dreams that seem to come true—often referred to as precognitive dreams or déjà vu—typically involves the brain's processing of memory, coincidence, and perception rather than true foreknowledge. Dreams combine fragments of daily experiences, thoughts, and emotions, and sometimes later events may appear to match these dreams simply by chance. When a real-life event resembles a dream, the mind may link the two, making it feel as if the dream predicted the future. This experience is relatively common and generally considered a normal function of memory and perception.

Saying things to shock others  Is it strange or categorically bad to say things that shock others knowingly, and enjoy their reaction?  I have a close friend who partakes in such behavior, and I want to understand his motivation a bit better.  Some context  I am not speaking about an attention aspect, per se, since this satisfaction from saying "shocking things" would be usually in a personal relationship context, whether that be friendship, intimate, or familial.  Perhaps the idea stems from hurting others as a coping mechanism?  Further, the satisfaction is not sexual at all, but merely a satisfaction from the reaction to whatever was said.  I am currently investigating this idea, as I have seen my friend partake in such behavior, and often at that. This behavior is private, so I am doubtful that it is for attention. Some people knowingly say shocking things in private because they enjoy eliciting strong reactions from others. This can be linked to various motivations. It may provide a feeling of power or control, as provoking surprise or discomfort allows them to influence the emotional state of those around them. For some, it might be a way to test boundaries within relationships, increase intimacy by exploring unfiltered honesty, or even just seek novelty and stimulation. This behavior is not necessarily categorically bad or pathological, but it could be problematic if it consistently hurts others or undermines trust. Understanding your friend's specific motivations would require considering his personality, past

Is there a term for the desire to maintain a level of anxiety?  As a war veteran who has/had PTSD, I've noticed in myself a desire to maintain a level of anxiety and stress closer to what I was used to.  You've probably also heard/seen this type of behavior from other veterans who engage in High Risk Behavior after separation from service.  I've also known former incarcerated individuals and PhD graduates who do the same.  Is there an official term for this behavior? Further reading on the topic? Is it possible to over burden yourself?  If someone thinks a lot about their future like what are they going to do 1 year down the line or how will the live the rest of their lives and some of these ideas can be really extreme like taking a vow of celibacy in their thoughts. Can they end up over-burdening themselves. Also what are the symptoms of such a man/woman?  Thanks in advance! Is it possible to over burden yourself?  If someone thinks a lot about their future like what are they going to do 1 year down the line or how will the live the rest of their lives and some of these ideas can be really extreme like taking a vow of celibacy in their thoughts. Can they end up over-burdening themselves. Also what are the symptoms of such a man/woman?  Thanks in advance! Is it possible to over burden yourself?  If someone thinks a lot about their future like what are they going to do 1 year down the line or how will the live the rest of their lives and some of these ideas can be really extreme like taking a vow of celibacy in their thoughts. Can they end up over-burdening themselves. Also what are the symptoms of such a man/woman?  Thanks in advance! Yes, it is possible to overburden yourself by constantly thinking about the future and setting extreme expectations or restrictions, such as strict vows or rigid plans. This can lead to mental and emotional exhaustion. Symptoms of overburdening yourself in this way may include persistent anxiety, difficulty sleeping, chronic stress, feeling overwhelmed, irritability, trouble concentrating, and physical symptoms such as headaches or fatigue. If these symptoms persist, it may be helpful to seek support from a mental health professional.

Direct competition with others, such as seeing your friend's score and trying to beat it, generally increases motivation and performance for many people due to social comparison and the desire to win. Several studies on gamification and motivation suggest that competitive elements can lead to greater effort and achievement. However, this can depend on individual personalities. For most people, having visibility into a competitor’s score and striving to outperform them tends to produce higher scores compared to only competing against oneself. And if not what is it ?  Without enough knowledge in Psychology, I tend to think it is close to something like "dementia" / "paranoia" / "psychosis"  Any pointers ? Several cognitive biases can contribute to the tendency to believe that another person's guessed perception of you is true. One relevant bias is the 'illusion of transparency,' where people tend to overestimate how well others can read their thoughts or feelings. Another is the 'confirmation bias,' where people give more weight to information that confirms their existing beliefs about themselves. However, this specific logic does not correspond directly to a named cognitive bias; rather, it's a mix of misattribution, projection, and possibly mind reading (a cognitive distortion, not a psychological disorder). Is there a name for the phenomenon of perceived repeated interruption?  Is there a documented name for the phenomenon of repeated social interruptions. It's something I've discussed with others who seem to share the observation that often, when one is alone in a public space, others will consistently run into them.  One example is in single stall bathrooms. This is one that I've discussed with others, to much amusement: it seems that whenever I find an unattended public restroom, even in a secluded space away from population, more often than not someone will approach and knock within seconds.  Another would be driving along a typically empty road late at night, and having someone follow every single turn you take up until you get home, where they turn around and go from whence you both came, making seemingly no sense.  Obviously, this is a cognitive bias of some sort and likely not truly indicative of some social behavior. It reminds me of the spotlight effect, but the spotlight effect is defined as heightened self awareness, no? This seems to happen regardless of that, like there are people just waiting around to stumble upon oneself at the most inopportune times.  Paranoia might be one word for it, but talking with others anecdotally, it seems like most people have had this thought before. Is there a name for the phenomenon of perceived repeated interruption? Paranoia might be one word for it, but talking with others anecdotally, it seems like most people have had this thought before. The psychological phenomenon most relevant to your experience is known as the 'frequency illusion,' or the Baader-Meinhof phenomenon. This is a cognitive bias where, once you notice something for the first time, you tend to keep noticing it more often, making it feel like it happens frequently. In your examples (single-stall bathrooms, someone following your car), it’s likely your awareness of these coincidental interruptions makes them stand out more, even if they aren’t actually more common. Opposite of White Bear principle?  Is there any phrase/term to describe the opposite of White Bear principle (also known as ironic process principle)? I'm looking for a word to describe the process where something eludes you the more you try to think of it (or remember it). I think I may have come across a term to describe this phenomenon but I'm not sure if such a phrase/term exists. Opposite of White Bear principle? Is there any phrase/term to describe the opposite of White Bear principle (also known as ironic process principle)? I'm looking for a word to describe the process where something eludes you the more you try to think of it (or remember it). I think I may have come across a term to describe this phenomenon but I'm not sure if such a phrase/term exists. The psychological term for when you can't recall something the harder you try to remember it is called 'blocking.' This is when accessing certain memories becomes more difficult due to interference or repeated unsuccessful attempts to retrieve them. A common example is the 'tip-of-the-tongue' phenomenon, where a memory eludes you while you are actively trying to recall it.

Is there a special name for rejection of extremes in the list of cognitive biases?  Is there a special name for the cognitive bias that causes a person choose a compromise solution even the extreme solutions are better or causes person prefer a middle value even the extreme values are more suitable? Is there a special name for rejection of extremes in the list of cognitive biases?  Is there a special name for the cognitive bias that causes a person choose a compromise solution even the extreme solutions are better or causes person prefer a middle value even the extreme values are more suitable? Is there a special name for rejection of extremes in the list of cognitive biases?  Is there a special name for the cognitive bias that causes a person choose a compromise solution even the extreme solutions are better or causes person prefer a middle value even the extreme values are more suitable? Yes, the cognitive bias you are referring to is called the 'compromise effect' or 'extremeness aversion.' This bias causes individuals to avoid extreme options and favor the middle or compromise choice, even when an extreme option might be objectively better or more suitable for their needs. The compromise effect is a well-documented phenomenon in decision-making psychology and is often used by marketers to influence consumer choices.

How can someone enjoy something without dopamine?  Andrew Huberman says people and even animals with depleted dopamine levels can still enjoy stuff.  How does one enjoy without dopamine? Isn't it the pleasure chemical?  Can serotonin, oxytocin etc. contribute to pleasure experience like dopamine? How can someone enjoy something without dopamine? Can serotonin, oxytocin etc. contribute to pleasure experience like dopamine? How can someone enjoy something without dopamine?  Andrew Huberman says people and even animals with depleted dopamine levels can still enjoy stuff.  How does one enjoy without dopamine? Isn't it the pleasure chemical?  Can serotonin, oxytocin etc. contribute to pleasure experience like dopamine? Dopamine, serotonin, and oxytocin are all neurotransmitters, but they have distinct roles in the brain, particularly regarding feelings of pleasure or enjoyment. Dopamine is not the only chemical involved in the experience of pleasure. While dopamine is closely related to motivation, reward, and the anticipation of pleasure, actual feelings of pleasure (also called 'liking') can still be experienced even when dopamine levels are low. This is supported by research, including commentary from Andrew Huberman, showing that animals and people with depleted dopamine can still derive enjoyment from things—although they may lack motivation to seek them

The personality disorder often considered the 'opposite' of Histrionic Personality Disorder (HPD) is Avoidant Personality Disorder (AvPD). While individuals with HPD seek attention and approval and are uncomfortable when they are not the center of attention, individuals with AvPD are excessively sensitive to criticism, experience deep feelings of inadequacy, and go out of their way to avoid social interactions due to fear of rejection or being noticed. AvPD can be highly disruptive to a healthy lifestyle by leading to isolation. Social Anxiety Disorder (Social Phobia) also shares some similarities with this pattern. Antisocial Personality

I am learning instruments and am sometimes amazed at how hard a seemingly simple thing like raising two different sets of fingers after one another can be.  I wonder how the brain learns this? Is it the cerebellum and the motor cortex where the "cortical homonculus" is or is it a cooperation between even more brain regions? What part of brain learns/controls finger motion?  I am learning instruments and am sometimes amazed at how hard a seemingly simple thing like raising two different sets of fingers after one another can be.  I wonder how the brain learns this? Is it the cerebellum and the motor cortex where the "cortical homonculus" is or is it a cooperation between even more brain regions? What part of brain learns/controls finger motion?  I am learning instruments and am sometimes amazed at how hard a seemingly simple thing like raising two different sets of fingers after one another can be.  I wonder how the brain learns this? Is it the cerebellum and the motor cortex where the "cortical homonculus" is or is it a cooperation between even more brain regions? The coordination of complex finger movements involves multiple regions of the brain working together. The primary motor cortex (located in the precentral gyrus) is responsible for initiating voluntary fine motor movements and is heavily mapped for hand and finger control (the 'cortical homunculus'). The cerebellum plays a crucial role in refining these movements, helping with coordination, timing, and error correction. Other areas, like the premotor cortex and basal ganglia, also contribute to planning and smoothly executing movements. So, learning and controlling finger motion involves cooperation between the motor cortex, cerebellum, and additional brain

Can anyone point me to academic work that systematically studies how standards and methods have changed in psychology as a response to the replication crisis? Thanks. Several academic papers have systematically studied changes in psychological science methods and standards following the replication crisis. One widely cited analysis is 'Data sharing and the future of science' by Nosek et al. (2015), which discusses open data practices. Also, a 2018 paper by Chambers and Munafò, 'Trust in science would be improved by study pre-registration,' outlines the adoption of study preregistration and registered reports to improve transparency. Reviews such as 'The credibility revolution in psychology' by Vazire (2018) and 'A manifesto for reproducible science' by Munafò et al

There are some questions here about various optical illusions. I stumbled upon this one and would like to find out where does it belong. Wikipedia has a page about illusory motion that mentions several types; peripheral drift seems to be closest, but I am not sure about it. The peripheral drift illusion is a common explanation for motion illusions where stationary images appear to move, particularly when viewed peripherally. This effect usually arises from patterns with strong luminance gradients, color contrasts, or repeating elements arranged in a certain way. The illusory motion is caused by the way visual neurons process these contrasts and gradients, leading to delayed responses that your brain interprets as movement. If the illusion you’re referring to creates the appearance of motion without anything actually moving, and is strongest when seen with peripheral vision, it likely belongs to the category of peripheral drift illusions.

Why do fNIRS devices commonly use two different frequencies?  One of the most common techniques used for functional neuroimaging nowadays is functional near infra-red spectroscopy (fun fact: IIRC Natalie Portman worked on a research paper involving fNIRS as the modality), which shines near infrared light into the brain from a source to a detector (both called optodes) in a "banana" shape.  It's not uncommon to read that most of these devices, be they continuous wave (CW) or one of the two kinds that involve fast modulation, frequency domain (FD) or time domain (TD), require two separate frequencies to be emitted. For instance, NIRx explains it as follows on their website:  "For neuro-imaging applications it is by far most common to illuminate with two discrete wavelength, which is the minimum requirement to assess relative variations of both oxygenation states of the hemoglobin molecule independently."  Why is that the case?  I haven't delved into the intricacies of it, but no reason immediately jumps out at me. For instance, in the case of CW, the relative difference in intensity is all that matters, so why do we need two frequencies? Why do fNIRS devices commonly use two different frequencies? I'm curious about the main contributing factors behind the loss of our hearing range as we age.  Is this degradation primarily linked to the loss of gray or white matter in our brains, indicating a neurological issue, or is it predominantly associated with the wear and tear of some other biological input mechanisms within our auditory system? What are the biological reasons for hearing loss?  I recently stumbled upon an interesting online hearing test on YouTube, and it got me thinking about the root causes of hearing loss. I'm curious about the main contributing factors behind the loss of our hearing range as we age.  Is this degradation primarily linked to the loss of gray or white matter in our brains, indicating a neurological issue, or is it predominantly associated with the wear and tear of some other biological input mechanisms within our auditory system? Age-related hearing loss, also known as presbycusis, is primarily due to degenerative changes in the structures of the inner ear, especially the loss of hair cells in the cochlea that detect sound vibrations. Over time, these sensory cells become damaged or die, and they do not regrow in humans. Other factors include changes in the auditory nerve that transmits signals from the ear to the brain, and, to a lesser extent, changes in brain processing as we age. While some central (brain-related) processing declines can contribute, most age-related hearing loss is due to 'wear and tear

For example, when someone is experiencing negative affect for some reason and then proceeds to act as if their day, or even life, is ruined, and that this negative affective state is the new norm. Or when someone is experiencing positive affect for some reason and then naively believes that "it will always be like this," perhaps getting very distraught when the positive feelings eventually go away but at the same time not being able to see this pattern from the outside or take long-term steps to prevent this type of roller coaster dynamic from continuing to manifest.  I have consulted various psychology resources and literature online but have not been able to, again, find the term which accurately captures this concept. I've found the following terms, "emotional myopia", "emotional reasoning", "anosognosia", "presentism", "presentist bias", and "egocentrism", which I feel are fairly close. But, apart from "emotional myopia" and perhaps "affective presentism", I feel the concepts I have found are either too general (egocentrism, emotional reasoning) or incorrect (alexithymia, affective agnosia).  The specific term I have previously seen is a single greek or latin word and is very specific, like the term "anosognosia". Based on what I've read, I think these don't qualify as communication disorders, but are instead anxiety disorders. It may be developmental or acquired. Individuals may demonstrate one or any combination of communication disorders. A communication disorder may result in a primary disability or it may be secondary to other disabilities.  I can sort of understand why it would be useful to separate anxiety disorders from these, but I'm not very knowledgeable in psychology, so I was hoping someone could verify what the answer is. Communication disorders and anxiety disorders are distinct categories within the DSM-5 (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition). Communication disorders include conditions such as Speech Sound Disorder, Childhood-Onset Fluency Disorder (Stuttering), Language Disorder, and Social (Pragmatic) Communication Disorder. Social anxiety disorder and selective mutism, while they can affect communication, are classified as anxiety disorders in the DSM-5, not as communication disorders. Selective mutism is specifically listed as an anxiety disorder. So your understanding is correct: both social anxiety disorder and selective mutism are categorized as anxiety disorders and not

In Yoga and many other traditional Eastern disciplines, the Chakra Theory is quite prevalent and the Anahata or the Heart chakra/meridian is quite strongly associated with emotions.  What is the stance of modern medical science on this? Can the source of all emotions be explained completely in the right hemisphere of the brain through neuroscience? Or is there any scientific backing for the linkage of Heart to emotions? Or such a discovery might be possible in future? Current neuroscience research identifies several key regions of the brain—such as the amygdala, prefrontal cortex, and limbic system—as central to the generation and processing of emotions. While historically emotions were associated with the heart in many cultures and traditions, modern medical science attributes the source of emotions to the brain, not the heart. The heart's role in emotions is generally seen as physiological response (like heart rate changes) to emotional states generated in the brain, rather than the origin of emotions themselves. Information which is often also trivial: e.g., smoking causes cancer. In fact, some studies find that graphical warning labels in cigarette packages affect implicit smoking attitudes (especially among the new smokers). Although comparatively smaller, text labels (compared to no labels) are also shown to have a similar effect. Repeated exposures to familiar or seemingly trivial information can have significant effects on attitudes and behaviors over time. This phenomenon is often explained by the 'mere exposure effect,' where people tend to develop a preference for things simply because they are familiar with them.

Suppose Emma is a patient of psychiatrist Dr. Jones, in New York State. In the course of their visits, Emma mentions that part of the stress and anxiety she feels is because of the pressure her husband puts on her and his intense personality.  Dr. Jones wishes to explore this more so he invites Emma to bring her husband to a local art gallery exhibit opening where Dr. Jones plans to attend as well, so he could meet them and observe Emma's husband and his interactions with her.  Are there any clear ethical issues with Dr. Jones invitation? The American Psychiatric Association (APA) provides clear ethical guidelines regarding dual relationships. Inviting a patient and her husband to a social function in order to observe them outside of a clinical setting is generally considered unethical. Such actions can blur professional boundaries, create a dual relationship, and may compromise patient confidentiality and the therapeutic alliance. Instead, Dr. Jones should address any need to observe or interact with Emma's husband within the appropriate context of treatment sessions, such as inviting him for a family or couples session in a professional setting.

Term for how one's self-image improves when something they associate with, like a sports team or politician, does well?  I'm trying to understand the phenomenon of how one's self-image, self-confidence, or personal regard goes up because something they affiliate themselves with "does well." For example, someone whose confidence and self-regard goes up when their team wins, or a character they relate to in a movie succeeds, or a politician they like says something clever.  What is this called? Term for how one's self-image improves when something they associate with, like a sports team or politician, does well? For example, someone whose confidence and self-regard goes up when their team wins, or a character they relate to in a movie succeeds, or a politician they like says something clever.  What is this called? Term for how one's self-image improves when something they associate with, like a sports team or politician, does well?  I'm trying to understand the phenomenon of how one's self-image, self-confidence, or personal regard goes up because something they affiliate themselves with "does well." For example, someone whose confidence and self-regard goes up when their team wins, or a character they relate to in a movie succeeds, or a politician they like says something clever.  What is this called? The phenomenon you are describing is called 'Basking in Reflected Glory' (BIRGing). BIRGing refers to the tendency of individuals to associate themselves with successful others or groups, leading to an increase in their own self-esteem, self-image, or confidence. For example, fans often feel more pride and self-worth when their sports team wins, as if they themselves have achieved something.

What term can describe the feeling that a job just does itself?  Is there a term that can describe that a job, however exhausting it might be, just does itself? Meaning, for example, that all doubt concerning how you're doing a job, whether or not you should do the job instead of something else, or any doubt of the value you're creating is just not there? Yes, there is a psychological concept that describes this state: it is most commonly referred to as 'flow.' Flow is a term popularized by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, describing a mental state in which a person is fully immersed, focused, and involved in an activity to the point where they lose track of time and self-consciousness. In flow, doubts and distractions fade away, and it can feel as if the work is happening effortlessly or 'doing itself,' even if the task is demanding.

Are there results on the null hypothesis of the "surgeon operating on my son" question?  There is a common question used to demonstrate the power of implicit assumptions about gender roles in our society.  Including it here for reference: A man and his son had a terrible car accident and were rushed to the hospital. The man died on the way, but the son was still barely alive. When they arrived, a surgeon was called in to operate. Upon seeing the young boy, the surgeon said, “I can’t operate – this is my son.”  Most people have difficulty thinking of the possibility that the surgeon is the son's Mother.  This riddle strikes me as having elements of the priming experiments whereby subjects primed with certain words perform better/worse in word-stem completion tests, intermixed with the social expectations of our culture. Here the "priming" would be using the male "father", "boy", and "son" throughout the question but asking for a female "mother" as the answer.  I was trying to find results on some sort of "control group" experiment to try and isolate the societal expectations element. Something like subjects being asked the same riddle but with a mother and daughter in a crash and the father a surgeon, or where it's the father and daughter in a car crash.  The only alternative I've seen is about "mother and daughter in crash, father is nurse" which is again combining the two effects.  N.b. I'm expect the priming effect is probably small, I just wanted to know by how much. Are there results on the null hypothesis of the "surgeon operating on my son" question? What is the inverse of anthropomorphism?  Anthropomorphism is the attribution of human traits, emotions, or intentions to non-human entities. However, one often can observe the inverse happening as well. Namely the attribution of non-human entities’ traits, emotions, or intentions to human entities. For example the biography book about Winston Churchill is called “The Last Lion” or professional boxers being nicknamed “Pitbull”, “Tiger”,”The Hammer”, etc.  Or would this be just an extension of anthropomorphism in which humans attribute human traits, emotions, or intentions onto non-human entities and then relate them back to humans again? Because, in order to attribute non-human entities’ traits, emotions, or intentions onto humans, one would first need to attribute human traits, emotions, or intentions onto non-human entities? However, one often can observe the inverse happening as well. Namely the attribution of non-human entities’ traits, emotions, or intentions to human entities. For example the biography book about Winston Churchill is called “The Last Lion” or professional boxers being nicknamed “Pitbull”, “Tiger”,”The Hammer”, etc.  Or would this be just an extension of anthropomorphism in which humans attribute human traits, emotions, or intentions onto non-human entities and then relate them back to humans again? Because, in order to attribute non-human entities’ traits, emotions, or intentions onto humans, one would first need to attribute human traits, emotions, or intentions onto non-human entities? The inverse of anthropomorphism, where animal or non-human traits are attributed to humans, is often called zoomorphism. Zoomorphism involves describing or portraying humans with the characteristics of animals, such as strength, cunning, or ferocity. Examples include calling a skilled boxer a 'Tiger' or referring to Winston Churchill as 'The Last Lion.' This is distinct from anthropomorphism, as the direction of attribution is reversed: instead of giving non-humans (like animals or objects) human qualities, zoomorphism ascribes animal qualities to humans.

On talking people out of delusions  One psychatrist I know recently wrote, offhand: "I remember when I was a student I tried so hard to convince someone that they weren't royalty, hours of passionate debate, and it just did nothing."  This got me thinking - are there published descriptions of what it feels like to try and convince a person with a grandiose delusion that it's wrong? When this fails, what are their responses to arguments, etc.? I feel like I've read fictional descriptions of this scenario plenty of times in various fiction books, but maybe in reality it looks different and now like I'd imagine? There are indeed published descriptions by psychiatrists and psychologists about the experience of trying to dissuade someone from a grandiose delusion. Clinically, attempting to directly confront or argue someone out of a firmly held delusion is almost always unsuccessful. Patients with delusions often resist explanations that contradict their beliefs. When arguments fail, common responses from the patient include rationalizing the contradiction, dismissing the evidence or source, or interpreting the challenge as further proof of a conspiracy against them. Research and professional accounts suggest that the conversation can feel frustrating or futile to the clinician, as logical reasoning rarely impacts the delus

Whether "group polarization" and "social segregation" is the same?  In a paper of (Murase, 2019) use both terms "group polarization" and "social segregation".  The "group polarization" is explained here, and the author also documents about the "social segregation" as  The relationship between homophily and segregation has been recognized long ago.(...)In another approach to social segregation, opinion dynamics is used so that similar people can influence each other  I am wondering whether these two terms "group polarization" and "social segregation" are the same? Group polarization and social segregation are both phenomena that occur within social groups, but they differ significantly in their dynamics and outcomes. Group polarization refers to the tendency for members of a group to adopt more extreme positions or attitudes after discussing an issue as a group. Social segregation, on the other hand, refers to the physical or social separation of people based on characteristics such as opinions, beliefs, or demographics, often resulting in homogenous subgroups. While the two can be related—social segregation can intensify group polarization by limiting exposure to differing viewpoints—they are not the same concept.

I did a search about "cumulative advantage" but I still cannot not link the concept to this situation  Cumulative Advantage states that once a social agent gains a small advantage over other agents, that advantage will compound over time into an increasingly larger advantage. The effect is well known and is embodied in "the rich get richer and the poor get poorer". Cumulative advantage, also known as the 'Matthew Effect,' refers to the idea that individuals who have an initial advantage tend to accumulate more benefits over time, leading to increasing disparity. In the context of weak homophilic relationships, even a small initial preference for connecting with similar others means that those connections are slightly more likely to form. As these similar connections build up, they can create a self-reinforcing process: people in homophilic relationships have more opportunities, support, or information, which in turn makes it even more likely that similar connections will continue to form and strengthen over time. What is the name for this type of cognitive bias where you are easier on yourself when you describe your behavior, but harder on the second person and hardest on others? What is the name for this type of cognitive bias where you are easier on yourself when you describe your behavior, but harder on the second person and hardest on others? It's similar to the justification of your actions in certain situations e.g., when someone cuts you off in traffic, he's a jerk. But if you cut someone off it's because you are really in a hurry and it's justified.  But what I'm specifically looking for is the name of the phenomenon where the same behavior/quality of the first, second and third person are described by decreasing levels of charitability. The phenomenon you're describing is known as the 'actor-observer bias.' This cognitive bias leads people to attribute their own actions to situational factors (being more charitable to themselves—the actor), while attributing others' actions to their character or disposition (being harsher to others—the observer). When judging yourself, you consider your context; when judging others, you focus on their traits.

How is this related to the width, stiffness and mass of the basilar membrane? The basilar membrane’s width, stiffness, and mass vary along its length, and these physical properties determine where different sound frequencies are detected. At the basal (bottom) end, the membrane is narrower, stiffer, and lighter, making it most responsive to high-frequency sounds, which causes the traveling wave to reach maximum amplitude there. At the apical (top) end, the membrane is wider, more flexible, and heavier, making it most responsive to low-frequency sounds—so the wave peaks at the apex for low frequencies. There have not been human trials specifically using illicit or harmful addictive substances (such as cigarettes or drugs) to build positive habits through associative learning, primarily due to ethical and health concerns. Research ethics guidelines prohibit intentionally exposing participants to potentially harmful substances for habit-building purposes. While some studies in addiction science look at cue associations and reinforcement, these do not advocate or test the idea of using harmful substances to reinforce positive activities. Instead, researchers recommend safer and evidence-based approaches, such as using positive reinforcement (rewards like praise or small treats), habit stacking, or gamification to encourage habit formation.

How long does an area clean-up help it stay clean?  Sometime in middle or high school I remember coming across this popular thinking of, when an area or neighbourhood has some trash, it makes people think it's ok to litter there themselves, and the more trash there is, the more of an unspoken 'norm' littering becomes. However, the hope was that the reverse is also true, and if a neighbourhood's cleaned up, then it signals to people that littering is the anti-norm here.  But I have gone on trash cleaning walks around my neighbourhood in the past few months, and I can't say that that hope is totally founded. The street by my apartment building has trash reemerge with a vengeance within a week. It makes me think there's some discrepancy in how this is thought about. Like, it's harder to see during night-time, so it absolves you from confronting the results of trash on the grass, and the like. The current scientific consensus is that sexual orientation is shaped by a complex interplay of genetic, hormonal, biological, environmental, and possibly social factors. Studies show that genetics contribute significantly to sexual orientation—twin studies suggest some heritability—though no single 'gay gene' has been found. There is no scientific evidence that someone can be persuaded to change their sexual orientation. Laws against so-called 'gay propaganda' are not supported by scientific evidence, since being gay is not the result of exposure or persuasion, and such laws are widely regarded as discriminatory.

The end of a thought process in the brain is signaled by several interconnected mechanisms. Primarily, the prefrontal cortex plays a central role in organizing, shifting, and terminating ongoing thought patterns. When a thought process concludes, changes in neural activity—especially in the prefrontal cortex and its connections—help shut down the current firing pattern. Additionally, inhibitory neurotransmitters like GABA dampen neuronal activity, preventing constant reactivation of the same neural circuit. One of the most common examples of this bias, is the perception of human faces in clouds, cars and all kinds of other objects.  What I am looking for is a lower level misperception, which people are usually aware of on a higher, cognitive level. I.e., people either are, or can easily become aware of the fact that they are not actually seeing a face (or another familiar pattern). The scientific term for the tendency to perceive familiar patterns, such as faces or objects, in unrelated or random stimuli is 'pareidolia.' This is a type of misperception where people recognize meaningful images where none actually exist, like seeing faces in clouds or random patterns. Even though people can usually recognize on a cognitive level that there is no real face, the initial perception still occurs.

Bias distrusting area of expertise while implicitly trusting other domains?  I've run across descriptions of this bias before, but cannot find it right now... I checked Wikipedia's list of cognitive biases to no avail.  Basically, people working in some domain and having expertise in it, naturally tend to notice the problems in that area, such as incompetence of other people working in their field, bad policies and poor management decision making, etc, resulting in a disproportionate distrust of their own field of expertise. For example, healthcare workers are more likely to be vaccine hesitant because they distrust their own industry more than most due to personal experience with incompetence, mismanagement, politics, and corruption within their field.  However, this distrust does not carry over to other domains. Recurrent synapses and feedback (top-down) synapses are different types of connections in neural networks and neuroscience. Recurrent synapses refer to connections where a neuron's output is fed back into itself or into neurons within the same layer or circuit, creating loops within the same level. In contrast, feedback (top-down) synapses are connections where signals travel from higher processing layers or areas to lower ones, mediating top-down influences. The psychological effect you're describing is called 'apophenia.' Apophenia is the tendency to perceive meaningful connections or patterns between unrelated or random things. It is closely related to 'patternicity' and can also involve elements of 'confirmation bias' when someone seeks out or emphasizes information that supports their perceived connection, even if it's coincidental.

Are there studies on the effectiveness of fasting for overcoming substance addictions or any other addiction for that matter? Currently, there is limited scientific evidence supporting fasting as a primary or universally effective method for overcoming substance addictions. While some individuals report benefits from fasting, including increased willpower and mental clarity, most addiction treatment programs rely on evidence-based interventions such as behavioral therapy, counseling, medication, and support groups. There are no large-scale clinical studies showing that fasting alone can treat or cure addictions, and abruptly stopping substances (cold turkey) can be dangerous, especially for substances like alcohol or prescription drugs, which can cause life-threatening withdrawal symptoms. It is important to consult medical professionals for safe and effective addiction treatment.

Is it possible to memorize the sound of a certain pitch?  Those how have an absolute pitch can name the note when they hear it. For those who don't have this ability is it possible to remember a pitch of at least one note? Is it possible to memorize the sound of a certain pitch?  Those how have an absolute pitch can name the note when they hear it. For those who don't have this ability is it possible to remember a pitch of at least one note? Is it possible to memorize the sound of a certain pitch?  Those how have an absolute pitch can name the note when they hear it. For those who don't have this ability is it possible to remember a pitch of at least one note? Yes, it is possible for people without absolute pitch to remember the sound of a specific note, although it usually requires some practice or reinforcement. Many musicians without absolute pitch are able to memorize the pitch of a reference note such as 'A440' (the A above middle C) and use it to help them find other notes by relative pitch. This process is not as automatic or precise as absolute pitch, but with repeated exposure and training, people can remember and accurately reproduce the sound of at least one particular note.

The scientific term most commonly associated with this phenomenon is 'optimism bias.' Optimism bias is the cognitive bias that causes people to believe that they are less likely to experience negative events and more likely to experience positive events compared to others, even in the face of evidence or knowledge to the contrary. This bias leads individuals to assume their case will be the exception to general trends or risks, as in your examples regarding relationships or cult involvement.

A common error people make when associating unrelated events that happen close together in time or space is known as the post hoc ergo propter hoc fallacy ('after this, therefore because of this'). For example, believing that observing a black van in a faraway town is related to an unsolved killing simply because they occurred around the same time is an instance of this fallacy.

Changes in lifestyle or environment can affect both IQ and EQ, but they do not convert directly into each other. If someone chooses to live far from people and focuses mainly on solitary study, their EQ might decrease over time because emotional intelligence often develops and is maintained through social interaction and practice. However, this doesn't mean that EQ will turn back into IQ; instead, the skills related to understanding and managing emotions may decline due to lack of use, while analytical or academic skills could improve with focused practice. Basically, when you want something but don't get it, you downplay the importance of that thing.  I've mostly seen it depicted as "moody teens" using responses like "I didn't want to go to that party anyway", or "you can keep your stupid X, I didn't want it anyway". Yes, there is a specific psychological concept that describes this behavior, known as 'sour grapes.' This term originates from one of Aesop's fables, where a fox is unable to reach some grapes and subsequently convinces itself that the grapes were probably sour and not worth having anyway. In psychology, 'sour grapes' refers to the act of downplaying the value or importance of something you wanted but couldn't obtain, as a way to protect oneself from disappointment.

Research in psychology suggests that revenge can be triggered by perceived injustice, violations of social norms, or threats to self-worth. While anger often involves an immediate emotional response to a gap between expectation and reality, revenge is a more deliberate response that can be influenced by learned behaviors, personal experiences, and cultural attitudes toward justice and retribution. Researchers rarely use the phrase 'sense pleasing.' Instead, they employ specialized terms and jargon such as 'hedonic value,' 'pleasantness,' 'reward,' 'aesthetic appreciation,' 'sensory pleasure,' 'affective responses,' and 'multisensory integration.' Good subfields to start with are neuroaesthetics, affective neuroscience, psychophysics, and sensory psychology. These areas study how we perceive and evaluate sensory experiences, and how the brain processes pleasurable sensations. Searching for these keywords and fields in academic databases will yield more relevant research.

Psychologists differentiate between feelings and emotions to clarify the complex ways in which humans experience affect. 'Feelings' typically refer to the subjective, conscious experience of affect—what we internally sense or perceive. 'Emotions,' on the other hand, are broader and typically include not just subjective feelings, but also physiological arousal (such as increased heart rate), cognitive processes (such as appraisal and interpretation of the situation), and behavioral reactions (such as facial expressions or actions). Including these factors in the definition of emotion makes sense because emotions are whole-body responses—your brain, body, and behavior all work together in response

IQ scores are designed to approximate a normal distribution through the process of test development and standardization. Test creators administer the IQ test to a large, representative sample and use statistical techniques to select or revise questions so that the overall scores closely follow a normal curve. The distribution often appears truncated because practical and theoretical limits are imposed—scores below a certain lower bound or above a certain upper bound are extremely rare or impossible due to limitations in both human cognitive ability and the design of the test. Several psychological terms describe the human tendency to mimic the actions of a group, especially under threat or uncertainty. The most common term is 'herd behavior' or 'herd mentality.' This refers to individuals in a group acting collectively without centralized direction, often following others' actions—such as running when others run or looking up when others look up—sometimes without understanding the reason. Another relevant term is 'social contagion,' which describes how behaviors and emotions can spread rapidly through a group. Both concepts can be referenced in your post to describe this phenomenon.

I tried googling this but couldn't come up with any discussion of this. The phenomenon you describe is sometimes called 'belief congruence bias' or 'similarity-attraction effect.' While 'attractiveness bias' usually refers to the idea that attractive people are judged as having better qualities, in the reverse direction, people can perceive those who share their beliefs as more physically attractive. Studies in social psychology have shown that perceived similarity, including shared political or religious views, can influence how attractive we find others. This is a well-documented effect, especially in the context of dating and friendships.

Is there a name for this phenomenon and research supporting it? Yes, humans often perceive a $99 price tag as significantly lower than $100 due to a psychological phenomenon known as 'charm pricing' or 'psychological pricing.' This effect occurs because people tend to focus on the left-most digits when reading prices, so $99 feels meaningfully cheaper than $100, even though the difference is only one dollar. Research in behavioral economics and marketing, including studies referenced in books like 'Priceless' by William Poundstone, supports that charm pricing increases sales by making prices appear lower to consumers.

Impaired verbal communication, but normal reading and writing skills?  Is there any cognitive disability or disfunction that impairs both speech production and listening comprehension, while reading and writing skills are at a normal level or better? Is there any cognitive disability or disfunction that impairs both speech production and listening comprehension, while reading and writing skills are at a normal level or better? Impaired verbal communication, but normal reading and writing skills?  Is there any cognitive disability or disfunction that impairs both speech production and listening comprehension, while reading and writing skills are at a normal level or better? Yes, there is a condition known as auditory-verbal agnosia (also called pure word deafness), where individuals have impaired speech comprehension and production due to difficulties processing spoken language, but their reading and writing abilities remain intact. This condition results from damage to the auditory regions of the brain, disconnecting the comprehension of spoken language from intact written language skills. Patients can often read and write normally, but struggle significantly with understanding or producing spoken language.

Or can we maintain our intelligence, working memory, and processing speeds for a longer period of time if we do physical exercise, keep physically fit, and live healthy lives? There is substantial research on strategies and exercises that can help adults improve their working memory. Approaches include cognitive training (such as memory games and tasks that challenge your short-term recall), maintaining a physically active lifestyle, managing stress, maintaining good sleep hygiene, and keeping a healthy diet. Additionally, engaging in lifelong learning and intellectually stimulating activities can help maintain and even improve cognitive functions, including working memory, as we age. While aging can lead to declines in processing speed and working memory, adopting healthy lifestyle choices may help slow this decline and maintain cognitive health.

Psychology looks at mental processes and behaviors—things like emotions, thoughts, and how people interact with the world. Neuroscience, on the other hand, studies the brain and nervous system—the physical and biological structures that create those thoughts and feelings. So, Psychology focuses more on the 'mind,' while Neuroscience focuses on the 'brain.

The term you may be looking for is 'experiential learning.' Experiential learning is a process where knowledge is gained through direct experience, practice, and reflection, rather than just theoretical understanding or rote memorization. This type of learning often involves engaging with real-life situations or hands-on activities, which helps embed concepts at a subconscious or intuitive level. Famous educational theorists like David Kolb have explored this, with Kolb's Experiential Learning Model highlighting the importance of concrete experience, reflective observation, abstract conceptualization, and active experimentation in the learning process.

The phenomenon where people remember negative experiences more vividly than positive ones is commonly referred to as the 'negativity bias.' This psychological bias means that negative events have a greater impact on a person's memory and perceptions than positive events of the same intensity.

What is the name of this effect? This effect is known as shifting the 'Overton Window.' Politicians may propose extreme or illogical ideas to move the boundaries of what is considered acceptable discussion, making previously radical views appear more normal or mainstream by comparison.

In the same way that there are theories of physics which do not presuppose the existence of a luminiferous aether, is the sort of thing that I mean.  Can there be, or is it impossible by definition? Several philosophical perspectives have challenged or outright denied the existence of the mind as a separate entity. For example, behaviorism in psychology focuses strictly on observable behavior and external stimuli, without presupposing the existence of an inner 'mind.' Similarly, eliminative materialism in philosophy of mind suggests that common-sense mental concepts (like beliefs or desires) do not correspond to actual entities in the brain and may eventually be replaced by more accurate neuroscientific terms. These approaches do not assume the existence of a separate 'mind' and instead explain psychological phenomena through brain processes or behavior alone.

I have searched for this and this, but the results don't provide a direct and suitable enough answer. The psychological and neurological mechanisms that cause us to forget information under pressure are primarily related to stress and anxiety. When we are in a stressful situation, our bodies release stress hormones like adrenaline and cortisol. These hormones can temporarily disrupt the functioning of the prefrontal cortex—the part of the brain responsible for memory retrieval and executive function. As a result, even information we know well can become difficult or impossible to access in the moment. This is why, for example, you might forget your favorite book when someone asks unexpectedly. Once the stress subsides, your ability to recall the information usually returns.

Yes, it is possible for the same action to serve as both positive and negative reinforcement depending on the context and the animal's internal state. In the scenario described, administering a CNS depressant after lever pressing could be positive reinforcement if the drug is experienced as pleasurable (adding a desirable stimulus). Simultaneously, if the monkey is under duress, the drug's ability to alleviate stress can act as negative reinforcement (removing an aversive condition). The fear of visiting doctors or undergoing medical examinations is known as 'iatrophobia.' This is a specific phobia related to medical settings or healthcare professionals, and is not the same as hypochondria (illness anxiety disorder), which involves excessive worry about having a serious illness. People with iatrophobia may avoid necessary medical care despite understanding the risks, and this avoidance can have negative health consequences. Iatrophobia is recognized as a psychological disorder and can be treated with therapy and other interventions.

Why do people find fascination in a tabloid magazine, paparazzi magazine, gossiping or whether prince and kings are dating or not or if actors, actresses, singers having any scandalous affairs or not?  I find gossiping similar to character assassination.  I have no business in their lives.  So what's the psychological reason for the fascination with gossiping or scandalous life? Gossiping fulfills several psychological needs for individuals. It often serves as a form of social bonding, fostering a sense of belonging and shared group identity. People are naturally curious about the lives of others, particularly those of high status like celebrities or royalty, which can help individuals compare themselves to others and navigate social hierarchies. Additionally, gossip can provide entertainment, distraction from one’s own problems, and a way to process social norms and acceptable behavior. While gossip can sometimes lead to character assassination, its widespread appeal is rooted in these deep-seated psychological drives.

The experience of movement or directional sensation while mentally visualizing complex code structures and information flow may resemble a form of synesthetic or cross-modal experience.
